title: EL Injection via buildConstraintViolationWithTemplate with user-controlled error message
description: CronValidator.isValid() uses context.buildConstraintViolationWithTemplate(e.getMessage()) where e.getMessage() may contain user-supplied cron expression text. The javax.el (Expression Language) dependency is included, which means Hibernate Validator/Bean Validation evaluates EL expressions in template strings. An attacker can craft a cron expression containing EL payloads like ${7*7} or ${class.forName('...')} which will be evaluated when the validator builds the constraint violation message, leading to Remote Code Execution (RCE).
suggestion: 1. Never pass user-controlled data directly to buildConstraintViolationWithTemplate(). Instead, use a fixed template without user input, and add the user input as a message parameter via addMessageParameter() or similar API. 2. Alternatively, sanitize the exception message by escaping ${ to prevent EL evaluation. 3. If EL is not needed, remove javax.el dependencies to disable EL evaluation entirely.

title: Multiple exception messages embed user input before reaching EL sink
description: The data flow chain shows multiple points where user input is embedded in IllegalArgumentException messages before reaching the EL sink. FieldParser.parse() throws exceptions like 'Invalid expression: ' + expression and 'Missing values for range: ' + expression. CronParser.parse() wraps these with String.format("Failed to parse '%s'. %s", expression, e.getMessage()). CronValidator then passes the final message to buildConstraintViolationWithTemplate(), enabling EL injection through any parsing error path.
suggestion: Ensure all exception messages used with buildConstraintViolationWithTemplate() do not include raw user input. Use parameterized messages instead.
